title: Foundations from Scratch
description: Eight from-scratch implementations inspired by Andrej Karpathy's Zero-to-Hero series — micrograd, BPE tokenizer, nanoGPT, and more — built with raw Python and NumPy, no ML libraries.
details: This file walks through eight foundational ML implementations from scratch, inspired by Karpathy's Zero-to-Hero lecture series. Each implementation uses only Python/NumPy — no TensorFlow, PyTorch, or sklearn. Topics include: micrograd (autograd engine), numpy MLP (2-layer network), manual backprop derivation, softmax+CE from scratch, BPE tokenizer, CNN with im2col, nanoGPT (minimal transformer), and KV cache. Every piece of code is designed to fit on a single screen, be interview-ready, and reveal how deep learning frameworks work under the hood.

sections.title: micrograd — A Tiny Autograd Engine
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Each node in the computation graph knows its parents and stores its local gradient. Backward pass multiplies local gradients along all paths using the chain rule — this is automatic differentiation by backpropagation.

[Practical Workflow] Interview angle: micrograd demonstrates you understand autograd internals. Interviewers ask: "How does PyTorch compute gradients?" The answer: each operation stores a local gradient, and backward multiplies via chain rule. Be able to trace through a + b × c manually.

sections.title: MLP from Scratch with NumPy
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: You only need matrix multiplication, ReLU, and softmax to build a neural network. Everything else is optimization. This 2-layer MLP from scratch (~60 lines) trains on any classification dataset.

[Practical Workflow] Interview angle: A from-scratch MLP proves you know the full forward/backward pass. Key details: Xavier init (var=2/(fan_in+fan_out)), ReLU gradient is {0, 1}, softmax+CE combined gradient = probs - y. This is the most commonly tested ML coding pattern at L4 interviews.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np

class TwoLayerNet:
    def __init__(self, D, H, C, lr=0.01):
        # Xavier init: scale by sqrt(2/in) for ReLU
        self.W1 = np.random.randn(D, H) * np.sqrt(2.0 / D)
        self.b1 = np.zeros(H)
        self.W2 = np.random.randn(H, C) * np.sqrt(2.0 / H)
        self.b2 = np.zeros(C)
        self.lr = lr

    def forward(self, X):
        """X: (N, D), return probs (N, C) and cache for backward"""
        z1 = X @ self.W1 + self.b1         # (N, H)
        a1 = np.maximum(0, z1)              # ReLU: (N, H)
        logits = a1 @ self.W2 + self.b2     # (N, C)
        # Stable softmax: subtract max for numerical stability
        logits -= np.max(logits, axis=1, keepdims=True)
        exp_l = np.exp(logits)
        probs = exp_l / np.sum(exp_l, axis=1, keepdims=True)
        cache = (X, z1, a1, logits, probs)
        return probs, cache

    def backward(self, cache, y):
        """y: (N,) int labels, returns loss"""
        X, z1, a1, logits, probs = cache
        N = X.shape[0]
        # Combined softmax+CE gradient: dL/dlogits = probs - y_onehot
        y_onehot = np.zeros_like(probs)
        y_onehot[np.arange(N), y] = 1
        dlogits = (probs - y_onehot) / N     # (N, C)
        # Loss for monitoring
        loss = -np.sum(y_onehot * np.log(probs + 1e-8)) / N
        # Backprop through W2, b2
        dW2 = a1.T @ dlogits                   # (H, C)
        db2 = np.sum(dlogits, axis=0)          # (C,)
        # Backprop through ReLU
        da1 = dlogits @ self.W2.T              # (N, H)
        dz1 = da1 * (z1 > 0)                   # ReLU gradient
        # Backprop through W1, b1
        dW1 = X.T @ dz1                        # (D, H)
        db1 = np.sum(dz1, axis=0)              # (H,)
        # SGD update
        self.W1 -= self.lr * dW1
        self.b1 -= self.lr * db1
        self.W2 -= self.lr * dW2
        self.b2 -= self.lr * db2
        return loss

    def predict(self, X):
        probs, _ = self.forward(X)
        return np.argmax(probs, axis=1)

# Usage:
# net = TwoLayerNet(D=784, H=128, C=10, lr=0.1)
# for epoch in range(100):
#     probs, cache = net.forward(X_train)
#     loss = net.backward(cache, y_train)
#     acc = np.mean(net.predict(X_val) == y_val)

sections.title: Manual Backpropagation — 3-Layer Network
sections.description:
[Key] FAANG: "Derive the gradient of cross-entropy loss with softmax." Answer: dL/dz = ŷ - y. Memorize this — it's the single most important gradient in ML interviews.

[Intuition] Intuition: The gradient of softmax+CELoss simplifies to (prediction - target) because the log and exp cancel in the derivative. This is why cross-entropy + softmax is the default classification pair.

3-Layer Network: X → Linear(σ) → Linear(σ) → Linear(Softmax+CE)

Forward:
  z1 = W1·x + b1    a1 = σ(z1)
  z2 = W2·a1 + b2   a2 = σ(z2)
  z3 = W3·a2 + b3   ŷ = softmax(z3)
  L  = CE(y, ŷ) = -Σ yᵢ · log(ŷᵢ)

Backward (chain rule, right to left):
  dL/dz3 = ŷ - y                    ← combined softmax+CE gradient (THE result)
  dL/dW3 = dL/dz3 · a2ᵀ
  dL/db3 = dL/dz3
  dL/da2 = W3ᵀ · dL/dz3
  dL/dz2 = dL/da2 ⊙ σ'(z2)          ← σ'(z) = σ(z)·(1-σ(z)) for sigmoid
  dL/dW2 = dL/dz2 · a1ᵀ
  dL/db2 = dL/dz2
  dL/da1 = W2ᵀ · dL/dz2
  dL/dz1 = dL/da1 ⊙ σ'(z1)
  dL/dW1 = dL/dz1 · xᵀ
  dL/db1 = dL/dz1

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: For a 3-layer net with widths [784, 128, 64, 10], the total parameter count is 784×128 + 128 + 128×64 + 64 + 64×10 + 10 = 109,002 params. Backprop computes all 109,002 gradients in one forward+backward pass — roughly 2× the compute of a forward pass.

[Practical Workflow] Interview angle: Backprop derivation is the #1 ML interview topic. Memorize: dL/dz = ŷ - y for softmax+CE. Practice deriving the full 3-layer chain rule on a whiteboard. Know that the gradient of sigmoid is σ(1-σ), max at 0.25, which is why deep sigmoid nets vanish.

sections.title: Softmax + Cross-Entropy from Scratch
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Stable softmax is critical — exp(1000) overflows to infinity. By subtracting the max logit, all exponents are ≤ 0 so exp values are in (0, 1]. The combined gradient dL/dz = softmax(z) - y is why cross-entropy and softmax pair so cleanly.

[Practical Workflow] Interview angle: The combined softmax+CE gradient dL/dz = softmax(z) - y is the single most important gradient in ML. Be ready to derive it. The stable softmax trick (subtract max) shows numerical awareness — always mention it when implementing softmax.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np

def stable_softmax(logits, axis=-1):
    """Numerically stable softmax.
    Subtracting max prevents exp overflow.
    """
    shifted = logits - np.max(logits, axis=axis, keepdims=True)
    exp_vals = np.exp(shifted)
    return exp_vals / np.sum(exp_vals, axis=axis, keepdims=True)

def log_softmax(logits, axis=-1):
    """Log-softmax: more numerically stable than log(softmax())."""
    shifted = logits - np.max(logits, axis=axis, keepdims=True)
    log_sum_exp = np.log(np.sum(np.exp(shifted), axis=axis, keepdims=True))
    return shifted - log_sum_exp

def cross_entropy(logits, labels):
    """Combined softmax + cross-entropy loss.
    labels: int indices, shape (N,)
    Returns (loss, gradient_dlogits)
    """
    N = logits.shape[0]
    probs = stable_softmax(logits)
    # Combined gradient: this is the KEY result
    # dL/dz_i = p_i - y_i  where y is one-hot
    y_onehot = np.zeros_like(probs)
    y_onehot[np.arange(N), labels] = 1
    dlogits = (probs - y_onehot) / N
    # Loss
    loss = -np.sum(y_onehot * np.log(probs + 1e-8)) / N
    return loss, dlogits

# The combined gradient dlogits = probs - y_onehot is why
# CE+softmax is the default: clean gradient, no log/exp in backward!

# For single example (binary):
# L = -(y log p + (1-y) log(1-p))
# dL/dz = p - y  ← same formula, sigmoid instead of softmax

sections.title: BPE Tokenizer from Scratch
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: BPE finds the optimal subword units by iteratively merging the most common character pairs. Starting from 256 byte-level tokens, it greedily builds a vocabulary of size K by merging the most frequent adjacent pair at each step.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: GPT-2 uses a 50,257-token BPE vocabulary (256 byte tokens + 50,000 merges + 1 special token). The merge list is learned from ~40GB of text.

[Practical Workflow] Interview angle: BPE is the standard LLM tokenizer (GPT, LLaMA, BERT). Interviewers ask: "How does GPT tokenize text?" Answer: byte-level BPE with 50K merges. Know the whitespace handling trick and that BPE builds subword units via frequency merging.
sections.codeBlock:
import re
from collections import Counter

class BPE:
    def __init__(self, vocab_size=50000):
        self.vocab_size = vocab_size
        self.merges = {}    # (token_a, token_b) -> new_token_id
        self.vocab = {i: bytes([i]) for i in range(256)}  # byte-level start
        self.next_id = 256

    def _get_stats(self, ids):
        """Count adjacent pairs."""
        pairs = Counter()
        for i in range(len(ids) - 1):
            pairs[(ids[i], ids[i+1])] += 1
        return pairs

    def _merge(self, ids, pair, new_id):
        """Replace all occurrences of pair with new_id."""
        new_ids = []
        i = 0
        while i < len(ids):
            if i < len(ids) - 1 and (ids[i], ids[i+1]) == pair:
                new_ids.append(new_id)
                i += 2
            else:
                new_ids.append(ids[i])
                i += 1
        return new_ids

    def fit(self, text):
        """Learn BPE merges from text."""
        ids = list(text.encode('utf-8'))  # start with raw bytes
        while len(self.vocab) < self.vocab_size:
            stats = self._get_stats(ids)
            if not stats:
                break
            most_freq = stats.most_common(1)[0][0]
            new_id = self.next_id
            self.next_id += 1
            ids = self._merge(ids, most_freq, new_id)
            # Store merge and new vocab entry
            self.merges[most_freq] = new_id
            self.vocab[new_id] = self.vocab[most_freq[0]] + self.vocab[most_freq[1]]

    def encode(self, text):
        """Encode text using learned BPE merges (greedy)."""
        ids = list(text.encode('utf-8'))
        while len(ids) > 1:
            # Find the lowest-priority merge available
            stats = self._get_stats(ids)
            candidate = None
            for pair, new_id in self.merges.items():
                if pair in stats:
                    candidate = pair
                    break  # first-found = earliest merge
            if candidate is None:
                break  # no more merges apply
            ids = self._merge(ids, candidate, self.merges[candidate])
        return ids

    def decode(self, ids):
        """Decode token IDs back to string."""
        byte_chunks = b''.join(self.vocab[i] for i in ids)
        return byte_chunks.decode('utf-8', errors='replace')

# Usage:
# bpe = BPE(vocab_size=500)
# bpe.fit("hello world hello world hello")
# encoded = bpe.encode("hello world")
# decoded = bpe.decode(encoded)  # back to original text

sections.title: CNN Convolution from Scratch (im2col)
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: im2col trades memory for speed — the unfolded matrix is ~9× larger (for 3×3 kernels) but uses highly optimized GEMM (General Matrix Multiply) from BLAS libraries. Modern frameworks like cuDNN use this approach for fast convolution.

[Practical Workflow] Interview angle: im2col shows you understand the memory-compute tradeoff in system design. Key interview point: convolution is >90% of CNN FLOPs, so optimizing it via GEMM is critical. Know that modern frameworks (cuDNN) use Winograd for 3×3 and im2col for larger kernels.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from numpy.lib.stride_tricks import as_strided

def im2col(images, kernel_h, kernel_w, stride=1, pad=0):
    """Unfold image patches into columns for fast GEMM convolution.
    
    Args:
        images: (N, C, H, W) input tensor
    Returns:
        cols: (N * H_out * W_out, C * kernel_h * kernel_w)
    """
    N, C, H, W = images.shape
    H_out = (H + 2 * pad - kernel_h) // stride + 1
    W_out = (W + 2 * pad - kernel_w) // stride + 1
    
    # Pad if needed
    if pad > 0:
        images = np.pad(images, ((0,0), (0,0), (pad,pad), (pad,pad)),
                        mode='constant')
    
    # Use strided view to extract all patches efficiently
    strides = images.strides
    patches = as_strided(
        images,
        shape=(N, C, H_out, W_out, kernel_h, kernel_w),
        strides=(
            strides[0],          # batch stride
            strides[1],          # channel stride
            strides[2] * stride, # height stride (by step)
            strides[3] * stride, # width stride (by step)
            strides[2],          # kernel height stride
            strides[3],          # kernel width stride
        )
    )
    # Reshape to (N*H_out*W_out, C*kh*kw)
    return patches.transpose(0, 2, 3, 1, 4, 5).reshape(
        N * H_out * W_out, -1)

def conv_forward(images, kernels, bias, stride=1, pad=0):
    """2D convolution using im2col + GEMM.
    
    Args:
        images: (N, C, H, W)
        kernels: (F, C, kh, kw)  — F filters
        bias: (F,)
    Returns:
        out: (N, F, H_out, W_out)
    """
    N, C, H, W = images.shape
    F, _, kh, kw = kernels.shape
    H_out = (H + 2 * pad - kh) // stride + 1
    W_out = (W + 2 * pad - kw) // stride + 1
    
    # Unfold: (N*H_out*W_out, C*kh*kw)
    cols = im2col(images, kh, kw, stride, pad)
    # Flatten kernels: (F, C*kh*kw)
    W_flat = kernels.reshape(F, -1)
    # GEMM: (F, C*kh*kw) @ (C*kh*kw, N*H_out*W_out) -> (F, N*H_out*W_out)
    out = W_flat @ cols.T + bias.reshape(-1, 1)
    # Reshape: (N, F, H_out, W_out)
    return out.reshape(F, N, H_out, W_out).transpose(1, 0, 2, 3)

# Memory analysis:
# For 3x3 conv on (N=32, C=64, H=224, W=224):
#   cols shape: (32*222*222, 64*9) = (1,577,728, 576)
#   cols memory: ~1.58M * 576 * 4 bytes = ~3.5 GB (this is why im2col is memory-hungry)
# But GEMM is insanely optimized (cuBLAS: ~10 TFLOPS on A100)

sections.title: nanoGPT — Minimal Transformer Implementation
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: A GPT-style transformer is just repeating blocks of: 1) multi-head self-attention (each token looks at all previous tokens), 2) feed-forward network (each token thinks about what it saw), 3) residual connections around both, 4) layer normalization before each sub-layer.

[Practical Workflow] Interview angle: nanoGPT ties together attention, multi-head, causal masking, layer norm, and GELU. To show depth: explain why pre-norm is preferred over post-norm (gradient flow through residual path). The full forward pass is ~100 lines — a common take-home interview task.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np

def gelu(x):
    return 0.5 * x * (1 + np.tanh(np.sqrt(2 / np.pi) * (x + 0.044715 * x ** 3)))

def softmax(x, axis=-1):
    x = x - np.max(x, axis=axis, keepdims=True)
    e = np.exp(x)
    return e / np.sum(e, axis=axis, keepdims=True)

def layer_norm(x, g, b, eps=1e-5):
    """x: (..., d), g,b: (d,)"""
    mean = x.mean(axis=-1, keepdims=True)
    var = x.var(axis=-1, keepdims=True)
    return g * (x - mean) / np.sqrt(var + eps) + b

class CausalSelfAttention:
    def __init__(self, d, n_heads):
        assert d % n_heads == 0
        self.d, self.h = d, n_heads
        self.dh = d // n_heads
        # Single QKV projection (concatenated)
        self.Wqkv = np.random.randn(d, 3 * d) / np.sqrt(d)
        self.Wo = np.random.randn(d, d) / np.sqrt(d)

    def __call__(self, x):
        """x: (T, d)"""
        T, d = x.shape
        # Project to Q, K, V: (T, 3d) -> split -> each (T, d)
        qkv = x @ self.Wqkv  # (T, 3d)
        q, k, v = np.split(qkv, 3, axis=-1)
        # Reshape for multi-head: (T, h, dh)
        q = q.reshape(T, self.h, self.dh).transpose(1, 0, 2)  # (h, T, dh)
        k = k.reshape(T, self.h, self.dh).transpose(1, 0, 2)
        v = v.reshape(T, self.h, self.dh).transpose(1, 0, 2)
        # Scaled dot-product attention
        attn = q @ k.transpose(0, 2, 1) / np.sqrt(self.dh)  # (h, T, T)
        # Causal mask: prevent attending to future tokens
        mask = np.triu(np.full((T, T), -1e10), k=1)
        attn = attn + mask
        attn = softmax(attn, axis=-1)
        # Weighted sum of values
        y = (attn @ v).transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(T, d)  # (T, d)
        return y @ self.Wo

class TransformerBlock:
    def __init__(self, d, n_heads, d_ff):
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention(d, n_heads)
        self.ln1_g = np.ones(d); self.ln1_b = np.zeros(d)
        self.ln2_g = np.ones(d); self.ln2_b = np.zeros(d)
        # FFN: expand 4x, GELU, project back
        self.Wff1 = np.random.randn(d, d_ff) / np.sqrt(d)
        self.bff1 = np.zeros(d_ff)
        self.Wff2 = np.random.randn(d_ff, d) / np.sqrt(d_ff)
        self.bff2 = np.zeros(d)

    def __call__(self, x):
        # Pre-norm: norm -> attn -> residual
        x = x + self.attn(layer_norm(x, self.ln1_g, self.ln1_b))
        # Pre-norm: norm -> ffn -> residual
        x = x + self._ffn(layer_norm(x, self.ln2_g, self.ln2_b))
        return x

    def _ffn(self, x):
        return gelu(x @ self.Wff1 + self.bff1) @ self.Wff2 + self.bff2

class NanoGPT:
    def __init__(self, vocab_size, d=128, n_heads=4, n_layers=4, d_ff=512):
        # Token and position embeddings
        self.token_emb = np.random.randn(vocab_size, d) / np.sqrt(d)
        self.pos_emb = np.random.randn(1024, d) / np.sqrt(d)
        # Decoder blocks
        self.blocks = [TransformerBlock(d, n_heads, d_ff) for _ in range(n_layers)]
        # Final layer norm
        self.ln_f_g = np.ones(d); self.ln_f_b = np.zeros(d)
        # LM head (tied or separate)
        self.lm_head = np.random.randn(d, vocab_size) / np.sqrt(d)

    def __call__(self, tokens):
        """tokens: (T,) int sequence. Returns logits (T, vocab_size)."""
        T = len(tokens)
        x = self.token_emb[tokens] + self.pos_emb[:T]
        for block in self.blocks:
            x = block(x)
        x = layer_norm(x, self.ln_f_g, self.ln_f_b)
        return x @ self.lm_head  # (T, vocab_size)

    def generate(self, tokens, max_new=100):
        """Autoregressive generation."""
        for _ in range(max_new):
            logits = self(tokens[-1024:])  # crop to max context
            probs = softmax(logits[-1])     # last token prediction
            next_token = np.random.choice(len(probs), p=probs)
            tokens = np.append(tokens, next_token)
        return tokens

# GPT-2 Small: vocab=50K, d=768, n_heads=12, n_layers=12, d_ff=3072

sections.title: KV Cache Implementation
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Without KV cache, generating token t recomputes attention over all t tokens — O(t²d) per step, O(n³) total for n tokens. The KV cache stores the Key and Value projections for each layer so that for each new token, we only compute Q_t (the query for the new token) and attend it against cached K,V.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: For n=4096, d=128, 32 layers: without cache = Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ i·2·d·h = ~275 GFLOPS per step (grows quadratically). With cache = 2·d·h per step = ~8 MFLOPS (constant). Savings: ~34,000× per decode step.

[Practical Workflow] Interview angle: KV cache optimization is the key to LLM inference efficiency. The 34,000× savings number is a great interview talking point. Know that memory-bandwidth-bound inference means FlashAttention (tiling) is more impactful than arithmetic optimizations for LLM serving.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np

def softmax(x, axis=-1):
    x = x - np.max(x, axis=axis, keepdims=True)
    e = np.exp(x)
    return e / np.sum(e, axis=axis, keepdims=True)

class AttentionWithKV:
    def __init__(self, d, n_heads):
        self.d, self.h = d, n_heads
        self.dh = d // n_heads
        self.Wk = np.random.randn(d, d) / np.sqrt(d)
        self.Wv = np.random.randn(d, d) / np.sqrt(d)
        self.Wq = np.random.randn(d, d) / np.sqrt(d)
        self.Wo = np.random.randn(d, d) / np.sqrt(d)
        # Cached K, V — cleared per sequence
        self.k_cache = []
        self.v_cache = []

    def clear_cache(self):
        self.k_cache = []
        self.v_cache = []

    def forward_with_kv_cache(self, x_t):
        """x_t: (d,) — single token embedding at position t.
        Returns: output (d,) for this token.
        """
        # Q for current token only
        q = (x_t @ self.Wq).reshape(1, self.h, self.dh).transpose(1, 0, 2)
        # K, V for current token
        k_t = (x_t @ self.Wk).reshape(1, self.h, self.dh).transpose(1, 0, 2)
        v_t = (x_t @ self.Wv).reshape(1, self.h, self.dh).transpose(1, 0, 2)
        # Append to cache
        self.k_cache.append(k_t)
        self.v_cache.append(v_t)
        k = np.concatenate(self.k_cache, axis=1)  # (h, t, dh)
        v = np.concatenate(self.v_cache, axis=1)
        # Attend Q against all cached K,V
        attn = q @ k.transpose(0, 2, 1) / np.sqrt(self.dh)  # (h, 1, t)
        attn = softmax(attn, axis=-1)
        out = (attn @ v).transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(-1)  # (d,)
        return out @ self.Wo

    def forward_no_cache(self, x):
        """Original full attention: x: (T, d). O(T²) both memory and compute."""
        T = x.shape[0]
        q = (x @ self.Wq).reshape(T, self.h, self.dh).transpose(1, 0, 2)
        k = (x @ self.Wk).reshape(T, self.h, self.dh).transpose(1, 0, 2)
        v = (x @ self.Wv).reshape(T, self.h, self.dh).transpose(1, 0, 2)
        attn = q @ k.transpose(0, 2, 1) / np.sqrt(self.dh)
        mask = np.triu(np.full((T, T), -1e10), k=1)
        attn = softmax(attn + mask)
        y = (attn @ v).transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(T, self.d)
        return y @ self.Wo

# Usage during inference:
# attn = AttentionWithKV(d=768, n_heads=12)
# for t in range(seq_len):
#     x_t = token_emb[input_ids[t]]  # one token at a time
#     out = attn.forward_with_kv_cache(x_t)
#     # Only O(d²) per step instead of O(t·d²)

# Key savings per decode step (for GPT-2 768d, 12 heads):
#   Without cache: 2 * T * d * h = 2 * T * 768 * 12 = 18432*T FLOPs
#   With cache:    2 * d * h = 2 * 768 * 12 = 18432 FLOPs (constant)
#   At T=1024: cache saves 1024× FLOPs per layer

sections.title: FAANG Interview Q&A
sections.description:
[Interview Angle] Q1: "What's the combined gradient of softmax + cross-entropy loss? Derive it." — Google L4+

Answer: dL/dz = softmax(z) - y_onehot. Derivation: L = -Σ y·log(p) where p = softmax(z). The derivative of softmax is p_i·(δ_{ij} - p_j). Combining with ∂L/∂p_i = -y_i/p_i and simplifying through the chain rule gives p_j - y_j. This cancellation is why CE+softmax is the default classification pair — the log and exp derivatives cancel perfectly.

[Interview Angle] Q2: "How does BPE tokenization work? What happens with whitespace?" — OpenAI L5

BPE starts with 256 byte-level tokens, counts adjacent pairs, and merges the most frequent pair into a new token — repeating until the desired vocabulary size. For whitespace: GPT-2 BPE adds a space-prefix trick — words with leading spaces are treated as subword units so "hello" and " hello" have different tokenizations. This preserves word boundaries without explicit whitespace tokens. Common pitfalls: BPE can split meaningful subwords ("unbelieveable" → "un" + "believe" + "able") but miss rarer patterns. SentencePiece and Unigram LM (used in Gemma, T5) are alternatives.

[Interview Angle] Q3: "Explain the im2col algorithm for convolution — what's the memory/compute tradeoff?" — Meta E5

im2col unfolds all convolutional patches into columns of a matrix: for input (N, C, H, W) with kernel (kh, kw), the output is (N·H_out·W_out, C·kh·kw). This matrix is ~kh·kw× larger than the input. The tradeoff: im2col consumes more memory (often 9× for 3×3 kernels) but enables using highly optimized GEMM routines from BLAS/cuBLAS, which operate near peak FLOPs on GPUs (~90% of theoretical peak on A100). The alternative is winograd or FFT-based convolution, which are memory-efficient but more complex to implement. Modern frameworks choose im2col for small kernels (≤5×5) and winograd for 3×3.

[Interview Angle] Q4: "What is the KV cache and how does it reduce inference cost?" — Google L5, OpenAI L5

The KV cache stores Key and Value projections from all previous decoding steps, avoiding recomputation. Without KV cache: generating token t requires O(t·d²) FLOPs per attention layer because Q, K, V are recomputed for all t tokens. With KV cache: only Q_t is computed for the new token, and K_cached, V_cached are reused — O(d²) per step. For GPT-3 175B with n=4096 context length, this reduces per-token FLOPs from ~1.6 TFLOPS to ~4 MFLOPS per layer. The memory cost is 2·n·d·l·bytes per sequence — for 4096 context, 128-dim, 96 layers, fp16: 2·4096·128·96·2 = ~200 MB per sequence. This is why MQA (Multi-Query Attention) and GQA (Grouped Query Attention) were introduced — they reduce K,V heads to lower cache memory.

[Interview Angle] Q5: "Derive the forward and backward pass for a 2-layer ReLU network with numpy" — Meta E4+

Forward: z1 = X·W1 + b1, a1 = ReLU(z1), logits = a1·W2 + b2, probs = softmax(logits). Backward gradient equations: dL/dlogits = (probs - y_onehot)/N (combined softmax+CE gradient), dW2 = a1ᵀ·dL/dlogits, db2 = Σ dL/dlogits, da1 = dL/dlogits·W2ᵀ, dz1 = da1 ⊙ [z1 > 0] (ReLU derivative), dW1 = Xᵀ·dz1, db1 = Σ dz1. The key insight: ReLU's derivative is 0 for negative inputs (dead neurons) and 1 for positive — so dead neurons never recover through gradient descent unless they receive positive input from upstream changes.

sections.title: References
sections.description:
• micrograd — Andrej Karpathy's tiny autograd engine: github.com/karpathy/micrograd
  • nanoGPT — Minimal GPT implementation: github.com/karpathy/nanoGPT
  • Andrej Karpathy "Let's build GPT" — YouTube series covering BPE, nanoGPT, micrograd from scratch
  • "Attention Is All You Need" — Vaswani et al. 2017: arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
  • Neural Networks and Deep Learning — Michael Nielsen: free online book with backprop derivation
  • CS231n notes — Stanford course: im2col, backprop, conv nets
